quote: it says to see if your ZIP code qualifies. That's what I was looking for.
stewart_read: This is a lead-premise mismatch — Mark's intent when clicking the ad was ZIP eligibility curiosity, not solar purchase intent. The rep's intro assumed purchase intent ('you were looking at solar') and never corrected the frame after Mark's clarification. The schema covers ad-premise complaints only obliquely via the intros section's legitimacy-establishment notes; there is no exact schema section for this scenario — this is a schema gap worth flagging for Kenny. The correct move here was to acknowledge the ad's framing, validate Mark's curiosity as the right starting point, and pivot to qualification as the natural next step ('That's exactly what this call is — let's see if your situation qualifies').
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep acknowledged the ad complaint and moved on deliberately, planning to qualify Mark before addressing the premise gap. Rejected — the rep never returned to the premise gap, and the qualification attempt was immediately derailed by the homeowner DQ.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to drill the lead-premise reframe. When a customer says 'I was just checking eligibility,' the rep's job is to validate that frame and make the call feel like the eligibility check the customer wanted — not a sales call they didn't sign up for. The rep who can make that pivot converts curiosity-clickers into qualified leads.
schema_reference: intros.standard (coaching_notes: 'Reference the form to legitimize the call')

quote: Oh, I see. Yeah. I know those ads are kinda crazy.
stewart_read: The rep's 'those ads are kinda crazy' is an attempt at rapport via commiseration — agreeing with the customer's implicit complaint about the ad's misleading framing. The problem is that it undermines Ion's legitimacy in the same breath it tries to build rapport. Commiserating about your own company's ad is not rapport repair; it's a credibility leak. The schema principle here is 'resolve via action, not defense' — the rep needed a legitimacy reframe, not a shrug.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the difference between commiseration ('those ads are kinda crazy') and legitimacy reframe ('That ad is actually accurate — the ZIP check is real, and that's exactly what I can confirm for you right now'). The second version validates Mark's intent AND re-establishes Ion's credibility in one move.
schema_reference: intros.standard (coaching_notes: 'Customer may be hostile about contact frequency — resolve via action, not defense')

quote: pretty much on his deathbed, and it's everything's in will and trust.
stewart_read: Mark discloses that his father-in-law is dying and the home transfer is tied up in estate proceedings. The rep's response is 'I see. I see. Okay.' — three filler acknowledgments with zero empathy content. Per the schema, a vulnerability disclosure is a trust gift, not a procedural interruption. The rep's non-response is the single moment that converts Mark from guarded-but-open to closed. The hard shutdown at 01:44 is a direct downstream consequence of this miss.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep kept the response brief to avoid dwelling on a sensitive topic and planned to pivot quickly to a practical next step. Rejected — the 'next step' offered (vague callback) was itself poorly constructed, and the brevity of the empathy response read as indifference rather than tact.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage coaching moment. Play the 01:16–01:44 sequence for the rep and ask: 'What did Mark just tell you, and what did he need to hear back?' The schema answer is a specific acknowledgment followed by a value bridge — something like: 'That's a lot to be dealing with — I'm sorry. When the time comes and the paperwork is sorted, the situation you're describing actually makes you a strong candidate. I want to make sure you're not starting from scratch when that happens.' That line keeps the door open without pressure.

stewart_read: Mark mentions his wife in passing. The rep never asks whether she is on the title or would be a co-decision-maker. On a conditional-DQ call where the goal is to preserve a future lead, this is a missed qualification anchor — if the wife is on the title (likely given ten-year co-residence), she is a required party for any future appointment. The rep had a natural opening here and walked past it entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep deprioritized title verification because the call was already DQ'd on homeowner status. Partially valid — but on a conditional-DQ call, title co-ownership data is exactly what you need to structure the future-touch correctly. Rejected as a full strategic justification.
coaching_implication: Even on a DQ call, the verify.title_co_owners item has value — it tells you who needs to be on the future appointment. Ask the rep: 'If you're going to call Mark back in six months, who do you need on that call?' The answer is probably Mark and his wife. The rep had the information in front of them and didn't capture it.

quote: What I could do is I could make a note here to maybe reach out to you in a a couple weeks and see if you had any luck with that.
stewart_read: The rep's callback offer is structurally weak on every dimension: no locked date, no locked time, no contact preference captured, no permission asked, and the framing ('maybe reach out') signals low commitment. The schema distinguishes sharply between callbacks with locked times and 'we'll touch base' — this is firmly in the latter category. The vagueness is what Mark reads as pressure rather than service, triggering the six-beat shutdown.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep kept the offer soft to avoid pressuring a sensitive customer. Rejected — softness in framing ('maybe,' 'kinda') is not the same as softness in pressure. A locked, permission-based re-engagement ('Can I reach back out in three months? I'll put it in my calendar so you don't have to think about it') is both softer in feel and harder in structure.
coaching_implication: Drill the conditional-DQ re-engagement framework: (1) ask permission explicitly, (2) lock a specific timeframe, (3) capture contact preference, (4) make the rep's commitment feel like service, not follow-up. The rep's current version does none of these. A suggested rephrase: 'I don't want to bother you during a hard time — would it be okay if I reached back out in a few months once things settle? I'll put a note so it's on me, not on you.'

quote: You're closing? We I've been living there for over ten years.
stewart_read: Ten-year co-residence at a property pending ownership transfer is a strong future-lead signal: Mark has deep roots at this address, is not a transient occupant, and has a clear path to ownership. The rep asks a clarifying question ('When are you moving in?') but does not anchor on the tenure as a qualification signal or use it to frame the future-touch value proposition. This is a lead-preservation opportunity the rep walked past.
coaching_implication: The ten-year tenure is the rep's best argument for why this lead is worth preserving. Coach the rep to anchor on it explicitly: 'Ten years — so this is your home, you're just waiting on the paperwork. That's exactly the kind of situation where it makes sense to have a plan ready so you're not starting from scratch when the title transfers.' That line converts a DQ into a warm future lead.

quote: No. No. No. No. No. No. When the time comes, I will contact whoever I will choose to do the solar panels.
stewart_read: The six-beat 'No' is the call's terminal signal — Mark is not just declining the callback, he is asserting vendor autonomy and closing the door on Ion specifically. This is the downstream consequence of two compounding failures: the empathy miss at 01:16 (which converted him from open to guarded) and the vague callback offer at 01:26 (which he read as pressure). Notably, Mark says 'I will contact whoever I will choose to do the solar panels' — he is not opposed to solar, only to this rep's approach. The lead is not dead; the rep's handling of it is.
coaching_implication: Use this line as the coaching anchor for the whole call. Mark is telling the rep exactly what went wrong: 'I will choose.' The rep's callback offer removed Mark's sense of control. Ask the rep: 'What could you have said at 01:26 that would have made Mark feel like he was in control of the next step?' That question surfaces the permission-based re-engagement framework without the manager having to lecture.
